DOC.label: Service Agreement
DOC.summary: Agreement governing the provision of services.

DOC.label: Master Services Agreement
DOC.summary: Framework agreement governing future statements of work.

DOC.label: Data Processing Agreement
DOC.summary: Agreement governing processing of personal data.

DOC.label: Lease Agreement
DOC.summary: Agreement governing the lease of property.

DOC.label: Purchase Agreement
DOC.summary: Agreement governing the purchase and sale of goods or assets.

CONCEPT.label: Confidentiality
CONCEPT.summary: Protection of confidential information.

CONCEPT.label: Notice
CONCEPT.summary: Formal communication required under the agreement.

CONCEPT.label: Services
CONCEPT.summary: Performance of contractual services.

CONCEPT.label: Return of Information
CONCEPT.summary: Return or destruction of confidential information.

CONCEPT.label: Consent
CONCEPT.summary: Formal permission or approval from a party.

CONCEPT.label: Breach
CONCEPT.summary: Failure to perform a contractual obligation.

CONSTRAINT.label: Prior Notice Required
CONSTRAINT.summary: An action may only occur after notice has been given.

CONSTRAINT.label: Cure Period Required
CONSTRAINT.summary: Termination cannot occur until the cure period has expired.

CONSTRAINT.label: Written Requirement
CONSTRAINT.summary: The action must be documented in writing.

CONSTRAINT.label: Authorized Signatory
CONSTRAINT.summary: Only an authorized representative may perform the action.

CONSTRAINT.label: Payment Deadline
CONSTRAINT.summary: Payment must occur before the specified due date.

EVENT.label: Contract Executed
EVENT.summary: The agreement has been signed and becomes effective.

EVENT.label: Cure Period Started
EVENT.summary: The period to remedy a breach has started.

EVENT.label: Cure Period Expired
EVENT.summary: The opportunity to cure has expired.

EVENT.label: Return of Information
EVENT.summary: Confidential information must be returned or destroyed.

EVENT.label: Force Majeure
EVENT.summary: Performance is excused due to extraordinary events.

EXCEPTION.label: Force Majeure
EXCEPTION.summary: Performance is excused because of extraordinary events beyond a party's control.

EXCEPTION.label: Mutual Consent
EXCEPTION.summary: Both parties agree to waive or modify an obligation.

EXCEPTION.label: Legal Requirement
EXCEPTION.summary: Disclosure or action required by applicable law.

EXCEPTION.label: Prior Written Consent
EXCEPTION.summary: Action allowed only after prior written approval.

ACTION.label: Pay
ACTION.summary: To give money to someone for something you want to buy or for services provided.

CONCEPT.label: Payment
CONCEPT.summary: Obligation to remit funds in exchange for goods or services.

CONCEPT.label: Termination
CONCEPT.summary: Core concept representing termination of the contract.

ACTION.label: Notify
ACTION.summary: Sending formal notice under the contract.

CONCEPT.label: Notice Email
CONCEPT.summary: Electronic mail transmission method for formal notice.

CONCEPT.label: Notice Address
CONCEPT.summary: Physical address block or destination details for formal notice delivery.

CONCEPT.label: Assignment
CONCEPT.summary: Transfer of contractual rights or obligations.

CONCEPT.label: Non-Disclosure / Confidentiality
CONCEPT.summary: Obligation on the receiving party to maintain the secrecy of Confidential Information and use it only for the permitted purpose.

RULE.label: CONCEPT_NON_DISCLOSURE
RULE.summary: Requires receiving party to maintain strict confidence.

CONCEPT.label: Indemnification
CONCEPT.summary: Core concept representing Indemnification.

CONCEPT.label: Broad Indemnity
CONCEPT.summary: Broad or unlimited indemnification obligations.

CONCEPT.label: Reciprocal Indemnity
CONCEPT.summary: Mutual or reciprocal indemnification provisions.

CONCEPT.label: Duty to Defend
CONCEPT.summary: Explicit duty of the indemnifying party to defend claims.

CONCEPT.label: Indemnity Exclusion
CONCEPT.summary: Exclusions or carve-outs from indemnification liability.

CONCEPT.label: Liability
CONCEPT.summary: Core concept representing Liability.

CONCEPT.label: Liability Cap
CONCEPT.summary: Limitation of liability or aggregate liability cap.

CONCEPT.label: Liability Exclusion
CONCEPT.summary: Exclusion of consequential, indirect, special, or punitive damages.

CONCEPT.label: Liability Carve-out
CONCEPT.summary: Carve-outs from liability caps (such as gross negligence, willful misconduct, or indemnity obligations).

ENTITY.label: Money
ENTITY.summary: Monetary values including currency symbol and amount.

ENTITY.label: Duration
ENTITY.summary: A length of time, typically expressed in days, months, or years.

DOC.label: Non-Disclosure Agreement
DOC.summary: Protects confidential information shared between parties.

PHRASE.label: Provided That
PHRASE.summary: Introduces a condition or proviso to a preceding obligation.

RULE.label: NOTICE
RULE.summary: Definitions are properly structured and present.

RULE.label: NOTICE
RULE.summary: Synonyms are successfully resolved to canonical entities.

RULE.label: NOTICE
RULE.summary: Capitalized term is used but not defined in the Definitions section.

CONCEPT.label: Force Majeure
CONCEPT.summary: Core concept representing performance excuse due to unforeseeable events.

RULE.label: OWNERSHIP
RULE.summary: IP ownership is clearly assigned to the creator/Disclosing Party.

RULE.label: OWNERSHIP
RULE.summary: No explicit assignment of created IP.

CONCEPT.label: Lifecycle
CONCEPT.summary: Core concept representing contract lifecycle.

RULE.label: LIFECYCLE
RULE.summary: An illegal contract state transition was detected.

node.name: Transfer

node.purpose: Determines if assignment is permitted and under what conditions.

node.text: Neither party may assign this Agreement without the prior written consent of the other party, except in connection with a merger, acquisition, or sale of substantially all assets.
Either party may freely assign this Agreement to any third party without notice or consent.

node.note: Anti-assignment clauses restricting delegation of duties (not assignment of rights) are generally enforceable; the UCC gap-fill default favors assignability.
A contractual prohibition on assigning payment rights (accounts) does not defeat a secured party's interest under UCC § 9-406.
Permits assignment of rights and delegation of duties absent contrary agreement, subject to material-change-of-risk limits.
Renders certain anti-assignment restrictions on payment rights (accounts) ineffective against a security interest holder.
General rule on assignability of contract rights.

node.purpose: Evaluates if a confidentiality obligation exists

node.text: The Receiving Party shall hold the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information in strict confidence and shall not disclose it to any third party except as required by law, with prompt notice to the Disclosing Party to allow it to seek a protective order.
The Receiving Party may use and share the Confidential Information at its discretion.

node.note: Trade secret claims may be brought under the DTSA alongside state law claims.
GDPR may independently apply where the confidential information includes personal data, in addition to trade-secret law.

node.rationale: Requires receiving party to maintain strict confidence.
node.recommendation: Ensure exceptions for legally required disclosures exist.

node.note: Federal trade secret protection that can run alongside a contractual confidentiality clause.
Adopted, with variation, by most U.S. states as the baseline for trade secret definitions and remedies.
Harmonized EU baseline for trade secret protection.

node.name: Non-Disclosure Agreement
node.description: Protects confidential information shared between parties.

node.name: Provided That
node.description: Introduces a condition or proviso to a preceding obligation.

node.purpose: Extracts definitions and assigns to canonical entities.

node.rationale: Definitions are properly structured and present.
node.recommendation: Review definitions for consistency with usage.

node.rationale: Synonyms are successfully resolved to canonical entities.
node.recommendation: Ensure aliases are consistent throughout the document.

node.rationale: Capitalized term is used but not defined in the Definitions section.
node.recommendation: Provide explicit definition for this capitalized term.

node.name: Force Majeure
node.description: Core concept representing performance excuse due to unforeseeable events.

node.text: Neither party shall be liable for delay or failure to perform due to acts of God, war, fire, flood, epidemic, or governmental action beyond its reasonable control, provided the affected party gives prompt written notice and uses reasonable efforts to mitigate.
Delays are excused only if approved in advance in writing by the other party.

node.note: Parties trading under the CISG should confirm whether Article 79 is excluded in favor of a bespoke force majeure clause.
Absent an express clause, excuse for goods contracts depends on the commercial-impracticability standard of UCC § 2-615, a materially higher bar than most negotiated force majeure clauses.
Excuses a party from damages for a failure due to an impediment beyond its control that could not reasonably have been foreseen.
Excuses timely delivery of goods where performance has been made impracticable by an unforeseen contingency.
Discharge by supervening impracticability.

node.recommendation: Ensure it aligns with the home jurisdiction of your entity.

node.name: Broad Indemnity
node.description: Broad or unlimited indemnification obligations.

node.name: Reciprocal Indemnity
node.description: Mutual or reciprocal indemnification provisions.

node.name: Duty to Defend
node.description: Explicit duty of the indemnifying party to defend claims.

node.name: Indemnity Exclusion
node.description: Exclusions or carve-outs from indemnification liability.

node.text: The Vendor shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the Client from and against any third-party claims arising from the Vendor's breach of this Agreement or negligent acts.
The parties shall be responsible for their own losses in all circumstances.
The Contractor shall indemnify the Owner against any and all claims, losses, and damages of any kind whatsoever, without limitation.
The Contractor shall indemnify the Owner for claims proximately caused by the Contractor's negligent acts or omissions.
Each party shall indemnify the other against third-party claims arising from its own breach of this Agreement.
Only the Client shall provide indemnification under this Agreement.
The Indemnitor shall defend the Indemnitee against any covered claim at the Indemnitor's own expense, using counsel reasonably acceptable to the Indemnitee.
The Indemnitor shall reimburse the Indemnitee's defense costs only after a final, non-appealable judgment.
This indemnity does not apply to claims arising from the Indemnitee's own gross negligence or willful misconduct.
This indemnity applies without exception, regardless of cause.

node.note: Anti-indemnity statutes (e.g., Texas Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 151, California Civil Code § 2782) void or limit indemnities for a party's own negligence, especially in construction contracts.
The express negligence doctrine (notably in Texas) requires indemnification of a party's own negligence to be stated in conspicuous, specific language to be enforceable.
Courts generally enforce mutual indemnities the same as one-sided ones, provided the obligations are genuinely symmetric.
Whether 'defend' creates an immediate duty (upon tender) or only a reimbursement duty (after judgment) varies by state and by the clause's precise wording.
Exclusions typically carve out claims arising from the indemnitee's own gross negligence or willful misconduct, and often interact with the liability cap.

node.text: arising out of or relating to

node.citation: Restatement (Third) of Torts: Apportionment of Liability § 22
node.note: General framework for contractual indemnity between parties.
Limits indemnification for a party's own negligence in construction contracts, a common target of overly broad indemnity language.

node.citation: Restatement (Third) of Torts: Apportionment of Liability § 22 cmt. b
node.note: Reciprocal indemnities are a common risk-balancing mechanism.

node.citation: Crawford v. Weather Shield Mfg., Inc., 44 Cal. 4th 541 (2008)
node.note: California case establishing that a duty to defend can be immediate upon tender of a claim, independent of the duty to indemnify.
Public policy limits on terms exempting a party from liability, relevant when drafting indemnity exclusions.

node.rationale: IP ownership is clearly assigned to the creator/Disclosing Party.
No explicit assignment of created IP.
node.recommendation: Ensure IP assignments are clearly written.

node.name: Liability Cap
node.description: Limitation of liability or aggregate liability cap.

node.name: Liability Exclusion
node.description: Exclusion of consequential, indirect, special, or punitive damages.

node.name: Liability Carve-out
node.description: Carve-outs from liability caps (such as gross negligence, willful misconduct, or indemnity obligations).

node.text: IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY'S AGGREGATE LIABILITY UNDER THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS PRECEDING THE CLAIM.
The parties' liability under this Agreement shall be as provided by applicable law, without further limitation.
Each party's total liability shall not exceed the total fees paid under this Agreement in the preceding twelve months.
No liability cap applies to either party under this Agreement.
Neither party shall be liable for any indirect, incidental, special, or consequential damages, including lost profits, even if advised of the possibility of such damages.
Both parties remain liable for all direct and indirect damages without limitation.
The foregoing limitation of liability shall not apply to damages arising from a party's gross negligence, willful misconduct, or breach of confidentiality obligations.
The liability cap applies to all claims without exception, including gross negligence.

node.note: The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 restricts exclusion of liability for negligence and imposes a reasonableness test on limitation clauses in the UK.
UCC § 2-719(2) invalidates a limited remedy that fails of its essential purpose, which can defeat an otherwise-enforceable cap in extreme cases.
Waivers of indirect/consequential damages are standard and generally enforceable, but are often carved back for confidentiality breaches or indemnified claims.
Most jurisdictions will not enforce a liability cap as applied to a party's own gross negligence or willful misconduct even if the clause purports to cover it.
Governs contractual modification or limitation of remedy, including liability caps and exclusions.
Confirms parties may agree to limit remedies, including by dollar cap, as long as the limitation does not fail of its essential purpose.
Limits recovery of unforeseeable damages, the doctrinal basis consequential-damages waivers reinforce by contract.

A term exempting a party from liability for harm caused intentionally or recklessly is unenforceable on public policy grounds, which is why carve-outs for gross negligence/willful misconduct are standard.

node.name: Lifecycle
node.description: Core concept representing contract lifecycle.

node.text: This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and continue for an initial term of one (1) year, automatically renewing for successive one-year terms unless either party provides sixty (60) days' notice of non-renewal.
This Agreement has no defined term or renewal mechanism.

node.note: Several states impose specific notice and disclosure requirements on automatic (evergreen) renewal clauses in consumer and some commercial contracts.

node.rationale: An illegal contract state transition was detected.
node.recommendation: Ensure state transitions are logically valid.

node.note: General definition of a contract and its lifecycle from formation through discharge.

node.name: Notice Email
node.description: Electronic mail transmission method for formal notice.

node.name: Notice Address
node.description: Physical address block or destination details for formal notice delivery.

node.text: Notice may be given by email to the address specified below and shall be deemed received on the next business day following transmission.
Notice by email is permitted with no further terms specified.
All notices shall be sent to the addresses set forth on the signature page, or to such other address as a party may designate by notice.
Notices shall be given as the parties see fit.

node.note: Absent an agreed deemed-receipt rule for email, disputes can arise over exactly when a notice period began to run.
A missing notice address is a common completeness gap that creates disputes over whether formal notices (e.g., of breach or termination) were properly given.

node.text: shall be sent to the address

node.note: General principles on when notice is treated as given/received, which contracts commonly adapt for electronic delivery.
Actual-receipt principles govern notice absent contrary agreement; a stated address removes ambiguity about where notice must be sent.

node.text: The Company shall pay all undisputed invoiced amounts within thirty (30) days of receipt, with interest accruing on late payments at 1.5% per month.
Payment shall be made whenever convenient for the paying party.

node.note: State usury statutes cap permissible interest rates on late payments; caps vary widely by state.
Default payment timing rules for goods contracts absent contrary agreement.

node.citation: Prompt Payment Act, 31 U.S.C. § 3901 et seq.
node.note: Prompt payment requirements applicable to certain federal government contracts and subcontracts.

node.text: Either party may terminate this Agreement for cause upon thirty (30) days' written notice if the other party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure within such period.
This Agreement may not be terminated by either party under any circumstances.

node.note: FAR-governed contracts follow a different termination-for-convenience and settlement framework than commercial agreements.
Ipso facto termination-on-bankruptcy clauses are frequently unenforceable in a bankruptcy proceeding under 11 U.S.C. § 365(e).
Reasonableness of cure periods and notice windows is assessed under general contract and good-faith principles.
Ipso facto clauses that terminate a contract solely due to bankruptcy filing may be unenforceable.
Federal government contracts generally include a standard termination-for-convenience right with settlement-cost recovery, distinct from commercial norms.
